title: Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy
oneLiner: Stress-induced transient left ventricular dysfunction mimicking acute coronary syndrome; predominantly in postmenopausal women with emotional or physical trigger.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute STEMI/NSTEMI** – look for: dynamic ST elevation with reciprocal changes, regional wall motion abnormality on echo (not the typical apical ballooning pattern). **Do not wait for angiography to treat ACS if high suspicion.**
- **Cardiogenic shock / acute LV failure** – look for: hypotension, raised JVP, pulmonary oedema, cool peripheries, NEWS2 ≥5. Will need urgent echo and cardiac ICU input.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Acute chest pain (central, pressure-like, often with dyspnoea)  
  - Dyspnoea (may be the dominant symptom)  
  - Palpitations  
  - Syncope or pre-syncope (less common)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Tachycardia / bradycardia (if arrhythmia)  
  - Hypotension or hypertension  
  - Signs of heart failure: raised JVP, fine crackles on lung auscultation, peripheral oedema  
  - A low-grade fever may be present (stress response)  
- **Typical trigger:** emotional (bereavement, fright, surprise) or physical (trauma, surgery, infection, acute medical illness) – but up to 30% have no identifiable trigger.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Acute coronary syndrome (ACS)** – ST changes, troponin rise, may show regional wall motion abnormality on echo; coronary angiography shows culprit stenosis.
- **Myocarditis** – often preceded by viral illness; elevated CRP, more diffuse ECG changes, cardiac MRI shows oedema/fibrosis.
- **Pulmonary embolism** – pleuritic chest pain, haemoptysis, hypoxia, raised D-dimer, CT pulmonary angiogram positive.
- **Aortic dissection** – tearing chest pain radiating to back, pulse deficit, widened mediastinum on CXR.
- **Sepsis with cardiac dysfunction** – evidence of infection, raised lactate, procalcitonin; echo shows global hypokinesis not ballooning.
- **Splitter:** Normal coronary arteries + apical ballooning on left ventriculogram or echo = Takotsubo vs. culprit stenosis/focal wall motion = ACS.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - 12-lead ECG immediately (ST elevation, deep T-wave inversion, prolonged QTc; may mimic STEMI)  
  - Continuous cardiac monitoring  
  - Capillary blood glucose  
- **Bloods:**  
  - Troponin (high sensitivity) – typically moderately elevated, with a slow rise and fall (peak < 10x upper limit)  
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs, CRP  
  - NT-proBNP (often very high reflecting acute wall stress)  
  - Arterial blood gas if hypoxic or shock  
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First-line:** Transthoracic echocardiogram (urgent) – shows typical apical ballooning or midventricular skinesis with basal hyperkinesis.  
  - **Second-line:** Left ventriculogram (during acute coronary angiography)  
  - Cardiac MRI (to rule out myocarditis, infarction, and confirm characteristic wall motion without late gadolinium enhancement)  
- **Diagnostic criteria (InterTAK Diagnostic Criteria):**  
  - Transient LV dysfunction (hypokinesis/akinesis) involving apical/midventricular segments  
  - Emotional or physical trigger (not mandatory)  
  - ECG abnormalities (ST/T changes, QTc prolongation)  
  - Mildly elevated cardiac biomarkers  
  - Absence of obstructive coronary disease (≥50% stenosis)  
  - No evidence of myocarditis or pheochromocytoma  
- **Never-miss test:** **Urgent coronary angiography** with left ventriculogram – omission risks missing STEMI and delaying revascularisation.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Manage as suspected ACS: give aspirin 300 mg PO (or [check local antiplatelet policy]), glyceryl trinitrate (GTN) 0.5 mg sublingual (if SBP >100 mmHg), IV morphine PRN for pain  
  - Urgent coronary angiography & left ventriculogram to confirm diagnosis and exclude culprit lesion  
  - If cardiogenic shock – involve cardiac ICU, inotropes cautiously (may worsen LV outflow tract obstruction – dobutamine preferred over norepinephrine if LVOT gradient present)  
  - Treat acute heart failure: IV furosemide if pulmonary oedema (dose per local policy), consider non-invasive ventilation (CPAP or BiPAP)  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Admit to cardiology ward or HDU/ICU if haemodynamically unstable  
  - Continuous cardiac telemetry for arrhythmia monitoring (significant risk of ventricular arrhythmias, torsade de pointes due to prolonged QTc)  
  - Daily ECG and troponin trend (troponin typically peaks within 24–48h)  
  - Repeat echo before discharge to document recovery of LV function  
  - Avoid stress and repeated catecholamine exposure (e.g., inotropes, beta-agonist inhalers)  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Usually full recovery of LV function in weeks to months  
  - Start heart failure therapy: beta-blocker (bisoprolol 1.25 mg OD, up-titrate) and ACE inhibitor (ramipril 2.5 mg BD, up-titrate) – even if LV function normalises early, to reduce recurrence  
  - Antiplatelet therapy not routinely continued (if coronary arteries normal)  
  - Consider psychiatric support if emotional triggers identified (e.g., bereavement, anxiety)  
  - Advise to avoid future extreme stress; no routine follow-up echo needed if normal at 3 months  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **IV fluids:** restrict if pulmonary oedema; otherwise 1 L/24h 0.9% saline (avoid excessive fluids if LVOT obstruction)  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** low molecular weight heparin (enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) if no contraindication and haemodynamically stable  
  - **Diet:** NBM until cardiac catheter performed, then light ward diet  
  - **Observation:** NEWS2 every 30–60 minutes initially, then hourly; continuous ECG monitoring
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**  
  - Bisoprolol 1.25–5 mg PO OD (start low, titrate to heart rate 50–60 bpm, avoid if acute heart failure/shock) – reduces sympathetic drive and LV outflow tract gradient  
  - Ramipril 1.25–2.5 mg PO BD (start low, titrate to BP, U&E monitoring) – afterload reduction  
  - Furosemide IV 20–40 mg (repeat as needed for pulmonary congestion) – use cautiously if LVOT obstruction  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Metoprolol tartrate 25–50 mg PO TDS (if bisoprolol not tolerated)  
  - Lisinopril 2.5–5 mg PO OD (alternative ACEi)  
  - Inotropes: Dobutamine IV (if cardiogenic shock with LVOT obstruction; avoid norepinephrine unless absolute hypotension) – [check local policy]  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Beta-blockers – avoid in acute asthma, bradycardia, hypotension  
  - ACEi – avoid in bilateral renal artery stenosis, hyperkalaemia, pregnancy  
  - Avoid NSAIDs if combined with ACEi/ARB (risk of acute kidney injury)  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - ACEi – reduce dose in CKD (eGFR <30); check U&E before and after initiation  
  - Beta-blockers – no major renal adjustment but use caution in severe hepatic impairment  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Inotropes** – giving high-dose catecholamine (e.g., norepinephrine, epinephrine) can worsen LV outflow tract obstruction and cause apical ballooning extension – use dobutamine as first line for shock with LVOT obstruction, and only after echo confirmation of no gradient. **Never give IV adrenaline/epinephrine in Takotsubo without echo guidance.**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Cardiogenic shock (5–10%)  
  - Acute LV outflow tract obstruction (due to hyperkinetic base) – echo required  
  - Mitral regurgitation (from papillary muscle displacement)  
  - Ventricular arrhythmias (VT, VF, torsade de pointes) especially with QTc >500 ms  
  - Apical thrombus causing systemic embolism (stroke, limb ischaemia) – anticoagulate if detected  
  - Rupture of LV free wall or septum (rare but fatal)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Recurrence (1–2% annually)  
  - Persistent LV dysfunction (rare; most recover within weeks)  
  - Anxiety disorder or post-traumatic stress related to the precipitating event
sections.Prognosis: - In-hospital mortality ~1–2% (higher if cardiogenic shock); most patients recover completely within 4–8 weeks. Long-term survival is good if precipitating cause removed. Recurrence rate ~1.5% per year.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always perform urgent echocardiogram** (with LVOT gradient assessment) before using inotropes in any patient with suspected Takotsubo and shock.  
- **Check QTc interval on ECG daily** – if prolonged (>500 ms), avoid QT-prolonging drugs (e.g., amiodarone, macrolides, antidepressants) and monitor for torsade.  
- **Start heart failure therapy (beta-blocker + ACEi) early** even if LV function normalises – reduces recurrence risk and mortality.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not assume Takotsubo just because of apical ballooning on echo without angiography** – you may miss a critical LAD/culprit lesion. Always proceed to coronary angiography.  
- **Do not give high-dose catecholamines (norepinephrine, epinephrine) in shocked patients without echo documentation of LVOT gradient** – they can worsen obstruction and cause clinical deterioration.  
- **Do not discharge without a repeat echo showing recovery** – LV function can deteriorate in the first few days.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Any recurrence of chest pain or breathlessness  
  - Palpitations, syncope, or near-syncope  
  - New leg swelling or inability to lie flat  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Cardiology clinic at 6–8 weeks for repeat echo  
  - If emotional trigger identified, consider psychology/counselling referral
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call cardiology senior / cardiac ICU if:**  
  - Hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg) despite fluids  
  - NEWS2 ≥5  
  - QTc >550 ms or any ventricular arrhythmia  
  - Echo showing LVOT gradient >50 mmHg or severe mitral regurgitation  
  - LV thrombus on echo  
- **Call crash team (2222) if:**  
  - VT/VF arrest  
  - Sudden loss of consciousness with no output  
  - Cardiac tamponade (hypotension + muffled heart sounds + raised JVP)

rawMarkdown:
## Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy
**One-liner:** Stress-induced transient left ventricular dysfunction mimicking acute coronary syndrome; predominantly in postmenopausal women with emotional or physical trigger.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute STEMI/NSTEMI** – look for: dynamic ST elevation with reciprocal changes, regional wall motion abnormality on echo (not the typical apical ballooning pattern). **Do not wait for angiography to treat ACS if high suspicion.**
- **Cardiogenic shock / acute LV failure** – look for: hypotension, raised JVP, pulmonary oedema, cool peripheries, NEWS2 ≥5. Will need urgent echo and cardiac ICU input.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Acute chest pain (central, pressure-like, often with dyspnoea)  
  - Dyspnoea (may be the dominant symptom)  
  - Palpitations  
  - Syncope or pre-syncope (less common)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Tachycardia / bradycardia (if arrhythmia)  
  - Hypotension or hypertension  
  - Signs of heart failure: raised JVP, fine crackles on lung auscultation, peripheral oedema  
  - A low-grade fever may be present (stress response)  
- **Typical trigger:** emotional (bereavement, fright, surprise) or physical (trauma, surgery, infection, acute medical illness) – but up to 30% have no identifiable trigger.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Acute coronary syndrome (ACS)** – ST changes, troponin rise, may show regional wall motion abnormality on echo; coronary angiography shows culprit stenosis.
- **Myocarditis** – often preceded by viral illness; elevated CRP, more diffuse ECG changes, cardiac MRI shows oedema/fibrosis.
- **Pulmonary embolism** – pleuritic chest pain, haemoptysis, hypoxia, raised D-dimer, CT pulmonary angiogram positive.
- **Aortic dissection** – tearing chest pain radiating to back, pulse deficit, widened mediastinum on CXR.
- **Sepsis with cardiac dysfunction** – evidence of infection, raised lactate, procalcitonin; echo shows global hypokinesis not ballooning.
- **Splitter:** Normal coronary arteries + apical ballooning on left ventriculogram or echo = Takotsubo vs. culprit stenosis/focal wall motion = ACS.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**  
  - 12-lead ECG immediately (ST elevation, deep T-wave inversion, prolonged QTc; may mimic STEMI)  
  - Continuous cardiac monitoring  
  - Capillary blood glucose  
- **Bloods:**  
  - Troponin (high sensitivity) – typically moderately elevated, with a slow rise and fall (peak < 10x upper limit)  
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs, CRP  
  - NT-proBNP (often very high reflecting acute wall stress)  
  - Arterial blood gas if hypoxic or shock  
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First-line:** Transthoracic echocardiogram (urgent) – shows typical apical ballooning or midventricular skinesis with basal hyperkinesis.  
  - **Second-line:** Left ventriculogram (during acute coronary angiography)  
  - Cardiac MRI (to rule out myocarditis, infarction, and confirm characteristic wall motion without late gadolinium enhancement)  
- **Diagnostic criteria (InterTAK Diagnostic Criteria):**  
  - Transient LV dysfunction (hypokinesis/akinesis) involving apical/midventricular segments  
  - Emotional or physical trigger (not mandatory)  
  - ECG abnormalities (ST/T changes, QTc prolongation)  
  - Mildly elevated cardiac biomarkers  
  - Absence of obstructive coronary disease (≥50% stenosis)  
  - No evidence of myocarditis or pheochromocytoma  
- **Never-miss test:** **Urgent coronary angiography** with left ventriculogram – omission risks missing STEMI and delaying revascularisation.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Manage as suspected ACS: give aspirin 300 mg PO (or [check local antiplatelet policy]), glyceryl trinitrate (GTN) 0.5 mg sublingual (if SBP >100 mmHg), IV morphine PRN for pain  
  - Urgent coronary angiography & left ventriculogram to confirm diagnosis and exclude culprit lesion  
  - If cardiogenic shock – involve cardiac ICU, inotropes cautiously (may worsen LV outflow tract obstruction – dobutamine preferred over norepinephrine if LVOT gradient present)  
  - Treat acute heart failure: IV furosemide if pulmonary oedema (dose per local policy), consider non-invasive ventilation (CPAP or BiPAP)  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Admit to cardiology ward or HDU/ICU if haemodynamically unstable  
  - Continuous cardiac telemetry for arrhythmia monitoring (significant risk of ventricular arrhythmias, torsade de pointes due to prolonged QTc)  
  - Daily ECG and troponin trend (troponin typically peaks within 24–48h)  
  - Repeat echo before discharge to document recovery of LV function  
  - Avoid stress and repeated catecholamine exposure (e.g., inotropes, beta-agonist inhalers)  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Usually full recovery of LV function in weeks to months  
  - Start heart failure therapy: beta-blocker (bisoprolol 1.25 mg OD, up-titrate) and ACE inhibitor (ramipril 2.5 mg BD, up-titrate) – even if LV function normalises early, to reduce recurrence  
  - Antiplatelet therapy not routinely continued (if coronary arteries normal)  
  - Consider psychiatric support if emotional triggers identified (e.g., bereavement, anxiety)  
  - Advise to avoid future extreme stress; no routine follow-up echo needed if normal at 3 months  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **IV fluids:** restrict if pulmonary oedema; otherwise 1 L/24h 0.9% saline (avoid excessive fluids if LVOT obstruction)  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** low molecular weight heparin (enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) if no contraindication and haemodynamically stable  
  - **Diet:** NBM until cardiac catheter performed, then light ward diet  
  - **Observation:** NEWS2 every 30–60 minutes initially, then hourly; continuous ECG monitoring

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**  
  - Bisoprolol 1.25–5 mg PO OD (start low, titrate to heart rate 50–60 bpm, avoid if acute heart failure/shock) – reduces sympathetic drive and LV outflow tract gradient  
  - Ramipril 1.25–2.5 mg PO BD (start low, titrate to BP, U&E monitoring) – afterload reduction  
  - Furosemide IV 20–40 mg (repeat as needed for pulmonary congestion) – use cautiously if LVOT obstruction  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Metoprolol tartrate 25–50 mg PO TDS (if bisoprolol not tolerated)  
  - Lisinopril 2.5–5 mg PO OD (alternative ACEi)  
  - Inotropes: Dobutamine IV (if cardiogenic shock with LVOT obstruction; avoid norepinephrine unless absolute hypotension) – [check local policy]  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Beta-blockers – avoid in acute asthma, bradycardia, hypotension  
  - ACEi – avoid in bilateral renal artery stenosis, hyperkalaemia, pregnancy  
  - Avoid NSAIDs if combined with ACEi/ARB (risk of acute kidney injury)  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - ACEi – reduce dose in CKD (eGFR <30); check U&E before and after initiation  
  - Beta-blockers – no major renal adjustment but use caution in severe hepatic impairment  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Inotropes** – giving high-dose catecholamine (e.g., norepinephrine, epinephrine) can worsen LV outflow tract obstruction and cause apical ballooning extension – use dobutamine as first line for shock with LVOT obstruction, and only after echo confirmation of no gradient. **Never give IV adrenaline/epinephrine in Takotsubo without echo guidance.**

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Cardiogenic shock (5–10%)  
  - Acute LV outflow tract obstruction (due to hyperkinetic base) – echo required  
  - Mitral regurgitation (from papillary muscle displacement)  
  - Ventricular arrhythmias (VT, VF, torsade de pointes) especially with QTc >500 ms  
  - Apical thrombus causing systemic embolism (stroke, limb ischaemia) – anticoagulate if detected  
  - Rupture of LV free wall or septum (rare but fatal)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Recurrence (1–2% annually)  
  - Persistent LV dysfunction (rare; most recover within weeks)  
  - Anxiety disorder or post-traumatic stress related to the precipitating event

### Prognosis
- In-hospital mortality ~1–2% (higher if cardiogenic shock); most patients recover completely within 4–8 weeks. Long-term survival is good if precipitating cause removed. Recurrence rate ~1.5% per year.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always perform urgent echocardiogram** (with LVOT gradient assessment) before using inotropes in any patient with suspected Takotsubo and shock.  
- **Check QTc interval on ECG daily** – if prolonged (>500 ms), avoid QT-prolonging drugs (e.g., amiodarone, macrolides, antidepressants) and monitor for torsade.  
- **Start heart failure therapy (beta-blocker + ACEi) early** even if LV function normalises – reduces recurrence risk and mortality.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not assume Takotsubo just because of apical ballooning on echo without angiography** – you may miss a critical LAD/culprit lesion. Always proceed to coronary angiography.  
- **Do not give high-dose catecholamines (norepinephrine, epinephrine) in shocked patients without echo documentation of LVOT gradient** – they can worsen obstruction and cause clinical deterioration.  
- **Do not discharge without a repeat echo showing recovery** – LV function can deteriorate in the first few days.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Any recurrence of chest pain or breathlessness  
  - Palpitations, syncope, or near-syncope  
  - New leg swelling or inability to lie flat  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Cardiology clinic at 6–8 weeks for repeat echo  
  - If emotional trigger identified, consider psychology/counselling referral

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call cardiology senior / cardiac ICU if:**  
  - Hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg) despite fluids  
  - NEWS2 ≥5  
  - QTc >550 ms or any ventricular arrhythmia  
  - Echo showing LVOT gradient >50 mmHg or severe mitral regurgitation  
  - LV thrombus on echo  
- **Call crash team (2222) if:**  
  - VT/VF arrest  
  - Sudden loss of consciousness with no output  
  - Cardiac tamponade (hypotension + muffled heart sounds + raised JVP)
